Version.Name: Total body elimination half-life (QSARINS)
Version.Summary: Quantitative model total body elimination half-life in human

Guide.Description: The is a multiple linear regression model based on Ordinary Least Square (OLS) that provide prediction for the total body elimination half-lives in human
Guide.Dataset: The model is based on data included in J.A. Arnot, T.N. Brown, F. Wania, Estimating screening-level organic chemical half-lives in human. Environ Sci Technol. 2014; 48:723-730. kT values (h-1) were transformed into LogHLT(h), then the dataset (n=1105) was split, before model development, into a training set used for model development and a prediction set used later for external validation; the splitting scheme was the same as the one used prevously by Arnot (n training=552 , n prediction=553). The range of LogHLTare -1.30 / 6.30 for the training set and -1.08 / 5.83 for the test set. After successful validation, the model has been retrained on the entire dataset for implementation.
Guide.insilico.dilibayer.Descriptors:
The model is based on the Ordinary Least Square – Multiple Linear Regression Methods (OLS – MLR) applied on LogHLT (total elimination half-life in human) data. The model is based on the following equation:
LogHLT= 0.6577 + 0.351 ScCl + 0.5905AATS7p - 0.0042 TopoPSA + 0.2105 nF - 0.0495 gmax - 0.4298 minsCl +0.0686 GGI1 - 0.2927 minsHsOH
The model uses the following descriptors:
[1]nF Number of fluorine atoms [2]minsHsOH minimum atom-type H E-state -OH [3]gmax maximum electrotopological state [4]SsCl sum of atom-type E-state -Cl [5]AATS7p average Broto-Moreau autocorrelation of lag 7 weighted by polarizabilities [6]TopoPSA topological polar surface area [7]GGI1 topological charge index of order 1 [8]minsCl minimum atom-type E-state -Cl
insilico.dilibayer.Descriptors were calculated based on canonical SMILES derived by OpenBabel (N.M. O'Boyle, M. Banck, C.A. James, C. Morley, T. Vandermeersch, G.R. Hutchinson, Open Babel: an open chemical toolbox. J Cheminform, 2011;3:33). The smiles string were used to calculate mono- and bidimensional descriptor by the software PaDEL-Descriptor (Yap C. PaDEL-Descriptor: an open source software to calculate molecular desctriptors and fingerprints. J Comput Chem. 2011). Constatnt descriptors and descriptors with a correlation greater than 0.98 were excluded from the total amout of descriptiors, using QSARINS software (Gramatica P., et al. QSARINS: A new software for the development, analysis and validation of QSAR MLR models, J Comput Chem (Software News and Updates). 2013, 34 (24), 2121-2132; Gramatica P., et al. QSARINS-Chem: Insubria Datasets and New QSAR/QSPR Models for Environmental Pollutants in QSARINS, submitted to J Comput Chem (Software News and Updates). 2013). The models were initially developed by the all-subset-procedure, and then GA was applied to obtain the final population of models (eight variables).

AD.Name: Similar_Molecules
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: strongly similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: only moderately similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: no similar compounds with known experimental value in the training set have been found

AD.Name: Accuracy
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is good
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not optimal
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: accuracy of prediction for similar molecules found in the training set is not adequate

AD.Name: Concordance
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that agree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that slightly disagree with the target compound predicted value
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: similar molecules found in the training set have experimental values that completely disagree with the target compound predicted value

AD.Name: Maximum_Error
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a low value, considering the experimental variability
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a moderate value, considering the experimental variability
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: the maximum error in prediction of similar molecules found in the training set has a high value, considering the experimental variability

AD.Name: Model_Descriptors_Range_Check
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: descriptors for this compound have values inside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: descriptors for this compound have values outside the descriptor range of the compounds of the training set

AD.Name: ACF_SimCheck
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: all atom centered fragment of the compound have been found in the compounds of the training set
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: some atom centered fragment of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: a prominent number of atom centered fragments of the compound have not been found in the compounds of the training set or are rare fragments

AD.Name: AD_Index
AD.DescriptionRangeTop: predicted substance is into the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeMid: predicted substance could be out of the Applicability Domain of the model
AD.DescriptionRangeBottom: predicted substance is out of the the Applicability Domain of the model

xml.Reference:
The model has been developed by:
[1]Alessandro Sangion University of Insubria (Varese - Italy) alessandro.sangion@uninsubria.it www.qsar.it [2]Lucrezia Motta DiSTA, University of Insubria (Varese - Italy) [3]Ester Papa DiSTA, University of Insubria (Varese - Italy) ester.papa@uninsubria.it www.qsar.it [4]Alberto Manganaro Kode srl info@kode-solutions.net
It has been described here: [1]S. Cassani, P. Gramatica, Identification of potential PBT behavior of personal care products by structural approaches. Sustain Chem Pharm, 2015;1:19-27 [2]Papa E., et al. Metabolic biotransformation half-lives in fish: QSAR modelling and consensus analysis, STOTEN. 2014;470-471:1040-1046 [3]A. Sangion, P. Gramatica, PBT assessment and prioritization of contaminants of emerging concern: pharmaceuticals. Environ Res, 2016a;147:297-306 [4]Yap, C.W. PaDEL descriptor: an open source software to calculate molecular descriptors and fingerprints., J. Comput. Chem. 2011 32, 1466-1474 [5]Gramatica P., et al. QSARINS: A new software for the development, analysis and validation of QSAR MLR models, J. Comput. Chem. (Software News and Updates), 2013, 34 (24), 2121-2132 [6]Gramatica P., et al. QSARINS-Chem: Insubria Datasets and New QSAR/QSPR Models for Environmental Pollutants in QSARINS, submitted to J. Comput. Chem. (Software News and Updates), 2013. [7]J.A. Arnot, T.N. Brown, F. Wania, Estimating screening-level organic chemical half-lives in humans. Environ Sci Technol, 2014; 48:723-730
For a general description of the VEGA software see: Benfenati E, Manganaro A, Gini G VEGA-QSAR: AI inside a platform for predictive toxicology Proceedings of the workshop "Popularize Artificial Intelligence 2013", December 5th 2013, Turin, Italy Published on CEUR Workshop Proceedings Vol-1107
xml.Endpoint: Total body elimination half-lives in human (HLT, h) expressed in base 10 log units (LogHLT)
